title: Inheritance, initial and computed values

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Inheritance, initial and computed values; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Cascading and Inheritance Level 5; W3C specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Inheritance, initial and computed values
sources.claim: Some computed properties inherit from the parent by default, while others use their initial value unless explicitly inherited.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Inheritance, initial and computed values
sources.claim: Some computed properties inherit from the parent by default, while others use their initial value unless explicitly inherited.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Some computed properties inherit from the parent by default, while others use their initial value unless explicitly inherited.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Setting color on an article reaches ordinary descendant text, but margin does not automatically propagate. Your goal is to trace a property's specified and computed value through an element tree. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: html document syntax; Specificity and source order; html document dom. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Some computed properties inherit from the parent by default, while others use their initial value unless explicitly inherited. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Inheritance differs from the cascade, custom properties inherit by default, and form controls can have user-agent styles. This draft therefore rejects: Every CSS property applied to a parent automatically applies to all descendants.

includes: Distinguish inherited, initial, unset and computed behavior including custom properties.
Trace a property's specified and computed value through an element tree.
excludes: Inheritance differs from the cascade, custom properties inherit by default, and form controls can have user-agent styles.

outcomes: Trace a property's specified and computed value through an element tree.

examples.title: Inheritance, initial and computed values: concrete case
examples.situation: Setting color on an article reaches ordinary descendant text, but margin does not automatically propagate.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Setting color on an article reaches ordinary descendant text, but margin does not automatically propagate.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Check each property's inheritance behavior; inheritable text properties differ from non-inherited box and layout properties.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Every CSS property applied to a parent automatically applies to all descendants.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Every CSS property applied to a parent automatically applies to all descendants.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Inheritance, initial and computed values, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Inheritance, initial and computed values before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
